exemplar.title: How does F. Scott Fitzgerald use characters in 'The Great Gatsby' to depict the American Dream as an illusion?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does F. Scott Fitzgerald use characters in 'The Great Gatsby' to depict the American Dream as an illusion?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Clear and consistent communication of topic and purpose throughout the essay
Research question is clearly stated, focused, and effectively connected to each major section
Headings mirror the research question and maintain reader focus
weaknesses: Methodology outlines basic steps but lacks explanation of source selection and systematic note-taking
Limited range of secondary sources reduces methodological robustness
Occasional awkward phrasing and overly long sentences slightly obscure clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (topic communication and research question) clearly meet the 5â6 band, while methodology fits the 3â4 band. Overall, the consistent clarity and strong RQ presentation justify a high band score of 5, despite the partial methodology.

strengths: Solid familiarity with The Great Gatsbyâs plot and historical context
Regular use of key psychoanalytic terms (id, ego, superego) and literary concepts like symbolism
Accurate primary quotations to support understanding
weaknesses: Engagement with secondary criticism is thin and often descriptive rather than integrated
Inconsistent terminology (variable capitalization of âsuperegoâ) and spelling errors
Application of scholarly sources lacks depth and effective synthesis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding strands both fall in the 3â4 band: good grasp of content and terms, but limited breadth and inconsistent application of sources. A mid-band score of 3 reflects this balanced performance.

strengths: Primary close readings are relevant and linked to the research question
Coherent argument linking identity, transformation, and conflict sections
Strong textual evidence especially in identity and transformation analyses
weaknesses: Reliance on a small body of secondary research, including general encyclopedia entries
Many paragraphs remain descriptive with only partially supported conclusions
Minimal critical evaluation of alternative interpretations or research limitations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Critical Thinking strands sit at the lower end of the 4â6 band: research is adequate but modest, analysis is partially supported, and evaluation is limited. A score of 5 reflects an overall solid but not consistently critical performance.

strengths: Core structural elements are present (introduction, sections, conclusion, reference list)
Headings guide the reader through the argument logically
In-text citations demonstrate awareness of scholarly conventions
weaknesses: Table of contents formatting is cluttered and hard to navigate
Page numbering is sporadic and inconsistent
Citation and reference formatting is mixed and does not follow a single style
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure meets basic requirements (band 3), but layout and formatting issues (band 1â2) indicate several presentation flaws. A score of 2 reflects an overall lower-band performance in Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay cites a limited range of secondary sources (two academic studies and two encyclopaedia articles) and relies heavily on primary quotations plus general background.  A psychoanalytic framework is announced, yet the method for selecting passages or critics is not explained and there is no sign of systematic note-taking or cross-comparison.  While the basic elements of EE methodology are present (word count, citations, reference list), the range and demonstration of informed selection are only partially convincing.

Basic layout featuresâtitle page, word count and in-text citationsâare present, but page numbers appear only sporadically, the table of contents over-runs a page with unreadable dots, and citation style is inconsistent (mix of MLA-like and web links).  Several IB formatting conventions (12-point font, double-spacing, uniform margins) cannot be verified.  These shortcomings place the work in the lower presentation band.

Primary research (close reading of Gatsby) is clearly relevant, and the few secondary sources chosen do relate to the question.  Nevertheless, the overall body of research is modest and some references (Britannica articles) add little analytical value.  The strand therefore fits the lower end of the âadequateâ band.

The student states the overall topic (disillusionment and the American Dream in The Great Gatsby) in the title page and reiterates it in both the introduction and the literature-review section.  Throughout the essay the same focus is maintained, with headings that mirror the wording of the research question.  Although phrasing is occasionally awkward, readers can easily identify what is being investigated and why.  Small lapses in clarity (e.g., very long sentences in the introduction) keep the work from the very top of the band, but the purpose and focus remain consistently clear.

A coherent line of argumentâlinking identity, transformation and conflict to disillusionmentâis maintained from introduction to conclusion.  However, evaluation of sources is minimal, and the final synthesis reiterates earlier points rather than offering a critical perspective on limitations.  The discussion is therefore good in places but not consistently critical.

The essay moves beyond plot summary by linking character actions to Freudâs model and the idea of a corrupted American Dream, and quotations are supplied for most claims.  Yet many paragraphs remain explanatory rather than analytical, and alternative readings or counter-arguments are seldom addressed.  Conclusions are only partially supported, placing the work in the adequate band.

The essay presents an introduction, thematic sections, conclusion and reference list, satisfying core structural expectations.  Headings guide the reader logically.  Nevertheless, the table of contents is badly formatted, some page numbering is irregular, and the flow within sections can be disjointed.  Hence the structure is appropriate but not flawless.

The student shows solid familiarity with the novelâs plot, historical context and psychoanalytic theory, quoting accurately from the text and summarising scholarly viewpoints.  However, engagement with secondary criticism is thin and often descriptive; for example, articles are named but their arguments are not fully integrated.  Knowledge is therefore good but not extensive or consistently well applied.

Key psychoanalytic terms (id, ego, superego, defence mechanisms) and literary concepts such as symbolism are used regularly and mostly correctly.  Nonetheless, some mis-spellings (ânarcissist traitsâ, âmerritt-cious beautyâ) and occasional confusion between âsuperegoâ and social conventions reveal partial inaccuracy.  Consistency and precision are adequate but not exemplary.

The research question â âHow does F. Scott Fitzgerald use characters in The Great Gatsby to depict the American Dream as an illusion?â â is explicitly placed on the title page, repeated in the introduction, and used to organise the body (identity, transformation and conflict of Gatsby, Daisy and Tom).  The question is sufficiently narrow for a 4,000-word study and is directly linked to every major section.  Minor drifting into contextual biography prevents a perfect score, but overall the RQ is clear, focused and well connected to discussion.

annotations.comment: The studentâs title clearly communicates the focus on disillusionment and the American Dream, establishing a strong, specific purpose for the investigation.
The research question is clearly stated on the title page and reiterated in the introduction, effectively anchoring each section to the studyâs focus.
The word count is provided correctly, adhering to IB formatting requirements and demonstrating attention to the investigationâs constraints.
The table of contents overruns the page with excessive dot leaders, making navigation difficult. Simplify entries and use consistent formatting.
Page numbers appear only sporadically in the footer. Ensure all pages are numbered consistently to aid reader orientation.
Consider reducing the length of biographical context in the introduction to maintain reader focus on disillusionment in the novel itself.
The citation of Britannica (Blumberg N, 2019) lacks consistency with other in-text references. Apply a uniform citation style (e.g., MLA or APA).
The overview of the American Dream definition is descriptive. Critically evaluate Adamsâs view and relate it to Fitzgeraldâs critique in the 1920s.
Terminology should be consistent: âsuperegoâ is variably capitalized. Standardize terms to avoid conceptual confusion.
Engage more deeply with secondary sources in the literature review: integrate their arguments rather than summarizing their conclusions.
Spelling of section header âJav Gatsbyâ is incorrect. Correct to âJay Gatsbyâ for clarity and professional presentation.
The identity and transformation section uses strong textual evidence. Quotations are well-chosen to support psychoanalytic interpretation.
The term âMerritt-curious beautyâ is misspelled and unclear. Verify original wording and correct to maintain precision in textual analysis.
Consider adding your own interpretative commentary after plot summary to deepen analysis and move beyond description.
Draw clearer links between the defence mechanism analysis and the central research question to reinforce focus and method.
The discussion of conflict remains largely descriptive. Introduce critical evaluation by exploring conflicting scholarly interpretations.
Paragraphs break mid-sentence, which interrupts the flow. Adjust paragraphing to improve readability and maintain cohesive argumentation.
Effective linkage of Gatsbyâs illicit actions to moral consequences highlights the psychological cost of his dream.
Daisyâs identity analysis offers strong insight into how societal expectations shape her decisionsâthis deepens the critical argument.
The Daisy identity section leans on summary; integrate critical perspectives or counterarguments to broaden analysis.
Linking Daisyâs cognitive dissonance to Freudian psyche and Zeldaâs biography is inventive, but the biographical parallel needs deeper justification.
Outline in the methodology section how characters were chosen and sources compared, strengthening the investigationâs transparency.
Tom Buchananâs conflict analysis is rich in example but lacks critique of critical interpretations; evaluate scholarly debates on his role.
The conclusion synthesizes main points but omits discussion of research limitations; adding this would strengthen evaluative depth.
Reference entries show inconsistent formatting and mixture of URLs and text. Adopt a single citation style and apply it uniformly.
Standardize the presentation of digital sources (underlining, italics, hyperlink format) to maintain professional reference list appearance.
